title: OpenRubrics: Towards Scalable Synthetic Rubric Generation for Reward Modeling and LLM Alignment

background.problem_context: Reward models are central to LLM alignment (RLHF, DPO), but traditional scalar reward models compress multi-dimensional response quality into a single number, losing interpretability and diagnostic power. Different prompts require different quality criteria (safety, accuracy, creativity), yet standard RMs apply a one-size-fits-all evaluation.
prior_limitations: Scalar reward models lack interpretability — cannot explain WHY a response scores high or low
Pairwise preference judgments lose information about which specific quality dimensions differentiate chosen vs rejected responses
Manual rubric creation is expensive and doesn't scale
Direct rubric generation (from prompt alone) often misses critical discriminative criteria
background.key_observation: Rubrics can provide structured, multi-dimensional evaluation criteria that are prompt-specific. When generated contrastively (by comparing chosen and rejected responses), rubrics capture the exact dimensions that differentiate good from bad responses. These rubrics can condition reward models to produce more accurate, interpretable, and decomposable reward signals.

motivation: Traditional reward models compress multi-dimensional response quality into a single scalar, losing interpretability and the ability to diagnose specific quality failures. Different prompts demand fundamentally different quality criteria. OpenRubrics proposes generating prompt-specific evaluation rubrics at scale, using contrastive generation from preference pairs to capture discriminative quality dimensions, then training rubric-conditioned reward models that provide structured, interpretable reward signals.
novelty_claims: OpenRubrics: first large-scale, open-source dataset of 15,000+ (prompt, rubric) pairs for reward modeling
Contrastive Rubric Generation (CRG): generates rubrics by contrasting chosen and rejected responses, producing rubrics with higher discriminative power than direct generation from prompts alone
Preference-label consistency filtering: self-supervised quality filter that retains only rubrics whose evaluations are consistent with ground-truth preference labels
RUBRIC-RM: rubric-conditioned reward model surpassing size-matched baselines by 8.4% on RewardBench and transferring to policy model training on instruction-following and biomedical tasks
problem_statement: Given a collection of (prompt, chosen_response, rejected_response) preference triples, generate high-quality, prompt-specific rubrics that can condition reward models to produce accurate, interpretable, multi-dimensional reward signals for LLM alignment.
core_idea: Generate rubrics contrastively: given a prompt and its chosen/rejected responses, ask an LLM to identify the specific quality criteria that distinguish them. This produces rubrics with high discriminative power. Filter rubrics via preference-label consistency: evaluate random responses using the rubric and check if the rubric's scores align with known preference labels. Train a rubric-conditioned reward model: input = (prompt, rubric, response) → structured reward score, enabling decomposable and interpretable evaluation.
extended_summary: OpenRubrics classifies rubrics into two types: Hard Rules (verifiable explicit constraints like format or language requirements, derived from prompt instructions) and Principles (implicit quality standards like coherence or creativity, requiring semantic understanding). Contrastive Rubric Generation (CRG) takes a preference triple (prompt, chosen, rejected) and asks an LLM to generate rubric criteria that explain WHY the chosen response is better. This is more informative than Direct RG (prompt-only), which often produces generic criteria missing the discriminative signal. Preference-label consistency filtering checks each rubric by evaluating both chosen and rejected responses: a valid rubric should consistently score the chosen higher. This self-supervised filter removes noisy rubrics that would introduce reward hacking. RUBRIC-RM is trained with input (prompt, rubric, response) → reward score. On RewardBench, the 2B RUBRIC-RM outperforms all size-matched baselines by 8.4%. The rubrics transfer to policy model training via DPO, improving instruction-following (IF-Eval) and biomedical QA tasks.

method_pipeline.name: Rubric taxonomy classification
method_pipeline.description: Classify rubric criteria into Hard Rules (verifiable constraints from prompt) and Principles (implicit quality standards)

method_pipeline.name: Contrastive Rubric Generation (CRG)
method_pipeline.description: Given (prompt, chosen, rejected), generate rubric criteria that explain why chosen > rejected. Uses LLMs (GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Gemma-2-27B)

method_pipeline.name: Preference-label consistency filtering
method_pipeline.description: For each rubric, evaluate multiple response pairs. Retain only rubrics where rubric scores are consistent with known preference labels (chosen > rejected)

method_pipeline.name: RUBRIC-RM training
method_pipeline.description: Train reward model conditioned on rubric: input = (prompt, rubric, response) → reward score. Architecture: Gemma-2-2B-it or 9B-it with rubric prepended to input

method_pipeline.name: Policy model training (downstream)
method_pipeline.description: Use RUBRIC-RM to generate preference pairs for DPO/RLHF training of policy models

design_choices.choice: Contrastive generation (CRG) rather than direct rubric generation
design_choices.rationale: Direct RG produces generic criteria that often miss the key discriminative dimensions. CRG leverages the contrast between chosen/rejected to identify exactly what matters

Two fundamentally different types of quality criteria: verifiable constraints (can be checked programmatically) vs implicit standards (require judgment). Separating them enables targeted evaluation strategies
Self-supervised: no human annotation needed. A good rubric should, by definition, score preferred responses higher. This elegantly filters noisy rubrics that would cause reward hacking

learning.supervision: supervised (preference data for rubric generation and RM training)
loss_functions: Bradley-Terry preference loss for reward model
learning.training_strategy: Two-stage: rubric generation (LLM prompting) → reward model training (fine-tuning)

quantitative_results.notes: Hard rules are most impactful for safety-related evaluations
Policy model trained with RUBRIC-RM preferences shows instruction-following improvement

baselines_compared.delta: CRG produces rubrics with significantly higher discriminative power
Filtering improves rubric quality and downstream RM accuracy

ablation_findings.effect: CRG produces significantly more discriminative rubrics, especially for subtle quality differences
ablation_findings.key_insight: Contrastive comparison exposes quality dimensions that prompt-only analysis misses

ablation_findings.ablation: With vs without preference-label consistency filtering
ablation_findings.effect: Filtering improves both rubric quality and downstream RM performance
ablation_findings.key_insight: 10-20% of generated rubrics are noisy and filtering prevents reward hacking

ablation_findings.ablation: Hard rules only vs Principles only vs Both
ablation_findings.effect: Both combined outperforms either alone. Hard rules dominate for safety; principles dominate for quality
ablation_findings.key_insight: The two rubric types capture complementary quality dimensions

main_findings: Contrastive generation produces significantly higher quality rubrics than direct generation by leveraging preference contrasts
Preference-label consistency is an effective self-supervised filter for rubric quality
Rubric-conditioned RMs outperform scalar RMs by 8.4% at the same model size
Rubrics provide interpretability: each reward score can be traced to specific criteria
Rubrics generalize across domains (instruction-following → biomedical QA)
limitations: Requires preference data (chosen/rejected pairs) for CRG
LLM-generated rubrics may inherit biases from the generator model
Rubric quality depends on the generator LLM's capability
Scalability limited by LLM inference cost for rubric generation

key_connections: OpenRubrics' contrastive generation inspires our rubric design from correct/incorrect trajectory pairs
Preference-label consistency filtering directly maps to our rubric validation: we check if rubric scores correlate with answer correctness
We adapt the rubric concept from response quality evaluation to routing quality evaluation
Our rule-based rubrics offer zero-cost scoring (vs LLM-as-judge), which OpenRubrics doesn't achieve
relevance_to_our_work.key_difference: OpenRubrics evaluates RESPONSE quality; our rubrics evaluate ROUTING DECISION quality. OpenRubrics requires LLM-as-judge for scoring; our rubrics are deterministic functions of trajectory features
